input_path: data\papers\Mertens and Denil - 2025 - Lab-Scale Gantry Crane Digital Twin Exemplar.pdf

extracted_characteristics.system_under_study: A lab-scale gantry crane system consisting of mechanical components and a controller, designed for research and educational purposes in digital twin applications. The physical system is a cyber-physical system where data flows bidirectionally between the physical crane and its digital counterpart to enable services such as optimal control, historical data logging, visualization, and continuous validation.
extracted_characteristics.dt_services: The Digital Twin provides services including optimal control for system optimization, historical data logging for recording operational data, data visualization for real-time monitoring, continuous validation to ensure model accuracy, and fault detection to identify system anomalies. These services are targeted at researchers and educators to support digital twin research and teaching, leveraging CAD models and kinematic models of the crane.
extracted_characteristics.tooling_and_enablers: The implementation utilizes Python with the gantrylib module for core logic, Docker for containerization of services, TimescaleDB for time-series data storage, Grafana for interactive data visualization, Eclipse Mosquitto as an MQTT broker for real-time data streaming, Autodesk Fusion360 for CAD model creation, and Capella for system architecture modeling.
extracted_characteristics.dt_constellation: Architecture visualized using C4 Model with context and container diagrams; employs containerization and virtualization technologies for deployment
extracted_characteristics.horizontal_integration: Uses MQTT protocol for real-time data exchange, TimescaleDB for time-series database storage, and Grafana for visualization dashboards, all containerized via Docker.
extracted_characteristics.dt_models_and_data: The Digital Twin utilizes CAD models for geometric representation and a kinematic model for motion simulation. Data sources include measurements of executed trajectories, with a Logging Application continuously recording the crane's state to a TimescaleDB database for management and analysis.
extracted_characteristics.physical_sensing_components: A Swing Angle Encoder measures the hoist's swinging motion, and an Anemometer provides wind speed measurements for environmental monitoring.
extracted_characteristics.fidelity_and_validity_considerations: A Continuous Validation Service compares executed trajectory measurements with simulations, calculates distance metrics between them, and evaluates the results against an acceptable threshold to ensure model accuracy and system reliability.
extracted_characteristics.physical_to_virtual_interaction: Data from the Physical Crane is collected by the Physical Crane Interface (Python program) and transmitted via MQTT to the Logging Application, which stores the data in a TimescaleDB database. Specific details on data types, transmission frequencies, data quality assurance, preprocessing steps, and integration mechanisms are not provided in the document.
extracted_characteristics.virtual_to_physical_interaction: Control commands generated by the Trajectory Generator Service are processed by the Control Application and sent through the Physical Crane Interface to the physical crane. The system uses an MQTT Broker for inter-service communication, but specific details on command types, message formats, validation mechanisms, feedback systems, conflict resolution, and command execution confirmation are not provided in the document.
extracted_characteristics.dt_technical_connection: The system uses an MQTT Broker for inter-service communication, TimescaleDB as the database, and Grafana for dashboard visualization. Specific details on network topology, bandwidth and latency requirements, reliability and redundancy mechanisms, and security protocols are not provided in the document.
extracted_characteristics.dt_hosting_deployment: The system is deployed on-premise on a personal computer using Docker Compose for container orchestration, as indicated by the provided docker compose file and Figure 5 depicting component deployment on a local machine.
extracted_characteristics.twinning_process_and_dt_evolution: The exemplars follow an ad-hoc development approach rather than commercial digital twin frameworks. The Air Quality Management exemplar uses Model-Driven engineering, while GreenhouseDT implements self-adaptive techniques. Version control is managed via GitHub repositories, and the lab scale gantry crane system's architecture is visualized using the C4 Model.
extracted_characteristics.standardization: The Air Quality Management exemplar utilizes AutomationML for digital twin modeling, a standard for industrial automation. The lab scale gantry crane system employs the C4 Model for architectural visualization, adhering to software architecture standards.

block_1_docs_retrieved:
# Lab-Scale Gantry Crane Digital Twin Exemplar



Joost Mertens

_Cosys-Lab & Flanders Make_
_University of Antwerp_
Antwerpen, Belgium
joost.mertens@uantwerpen.be


_**Abstract**_ **—The research topic of digital twins has attracted a**
**large amount of interest over the past decade. However, publicly**
**available exemplars remain scarce. In the interest of open and**
**reproducible science, in this exemplar paper we present a lab-**
**scale gantry crane and its digital twin. The exemplar comprises**
**both the physical and digital side of the twin system. The physical**
**side consists of the physical crane and its controller. The digital**
**side covers the CAD models and kinematic model of the crane,**
**and provides services for optimal control, historical data logging,**
**data visualization and continuous validation. We used this setup**
**as use case in several previous publications where its functionality**
**was validated. It is publicly available and only relies on other**
**freely available and commonly used software, this way we hope**
**it can be used for future research or education on the topic of**
**digital twins.**
_**Index Terms**_ **—exemplar, digital twin, open-source, cyber-**
**physical-system**


I. I NTRODUCTION

The digital twin paradigm connects an actual system with a
digital counterpart consisting of models of parts of the actual
systems and services derived therefrom. During operation
data flows from the actual to the digital system to feed the
twin’s services. In turn, the twin’s services improve the actual
system’s workings. While the paradigm is applicable for both
physical and digital actual objects [1], in practice we see it
mainly applied to physical ones [2], [3].
Because of this bi-directional interaction, demonstrating
research results requires relevant use cases. Such research
results could be new synchronization methods, better or faster
data streaming, improved services and the like. The exemplar
in this paper for example, was used as a demonstrator for the
following digital twin related research: continuous validation
methods [4], [5], fault detection methods [6] and DarTwin, a
notation for system evolution [7].
In our experience, the use cases themselves typically do
not contain new research, yet they demand a substantial development effort on the researcher’s part. Finding of-the-shelf
exemplars in literature is rare, as the use cases in papers are
often not described in a reproducible manner, are costly to set
up, or are not openly available because they contain intellectual
property. Having readily available off-the-shelf exemplars in
various domains would help reduce development overhead,
and additionally also also help support the generalizability of
research results.

Given the industry relevance of digital twins, education
is another field for which well described exemplars can be



Joachim Denil

block_1_judge.reasoning: The extraction accurately describes the lab-scale gantry crane, its components (mechanical and controller), and its purpose for research and education. It correctly identifies it as a cyber-physical system with bidirectional data flow enabling specific services. This is supported by the abstract describing 'the physical side consists of the physical crane and its controller' and the introduction stating it is 'a typical Cyber-Physical System' used for 'future research or education'.

The extraction correctly lists the specific services provided by the digital twin: optimal control, historical data logging, data visualization, and continuous validation. It also correctly identifies the target users (researchers and educators) and the models used (CAD and kinematic). This is strongly supported by the abstract listing 'services for optimal control, historical data logging, data visualization and continuous validation' and the container diagram descriptions in Section III.A. The mention of fault detection is supported by the text describing the system's use as a demonstrator for 'fault detection methods' [6].

The extraction is highly specific and detailed, perfectly mapping the tools to their roles as described in the implementation and repository sections. It covers the software stack (Python, gantrylib, Docker, TimescaleDB, Grafana, Eclipse Mosquitto) as well as modeling tools (Autodesk Fusion360, Capella). Quotes: 'bundled all the Python C4 Containers in a Python module named gantrylib', 'containerized with Docker', 'Database: a TimescaleDB database', 'Grafana dashboard', 'Eclipse Mosquitto', 'CAD models of the crane made in Autodesk Fusion360', 'Arcadia models made in Capella'.

block_2_docs_retrieved:
_B. GreenhouseDT_


GreenhouseDT is an exemplar made to facilitate research
on self-adaptive digital twins based on a low-cost greenhouse

[12]. It features behavioral adaptation of both the actual
system (the greenhouse) and the digital twin. The exemplar
and its accompanying documentation is hosted on GitHub [3] . It
relies on commonly used technologies such as an InfluxDB
database, an ActiveMQ message broker and virtual machines
for easy installation. The virtual machine images come with
prerecorded data.


_C. Air Quality Management_


The Air Quality Management exemplar was created to
research challenges in the field of Model-Driven digital twin
engineering [13]. It focuses on a construction engineering case
of indoor air quality management in the context of virus spread
prevention. Three example applications of the digital twin are
provided: runtime visualization, simulation and prediction. The
exemplar and instructions on how to set it up are hosted on
GitHub [4] . It features a Raspberry-Pi with a set of sensors for the
actual system and an AutomationML based digital twin [14].
Microsoft Azure tools are used to implement the presented
digital twin pipeline.


_D. Discussion_


The three exemplars demonstrate the use of digital twins
in the robotics, agricultural and construction domains. They
commonly use single board computers such as the RaspberryPi and containerization or virtualization technologies for easy
dissemination of the code. Interestingly, only one of them uses


2 [https://github.com/cau-se/ARCHES-PiCar-X](https://github.com/cau-se/ARCHES-PiCar-X)
3 [https://github.com/smolang/GreenhouseDT](https://github.com/smolang/GreenhouseDT)
4 [https://github.com/derlehner/IndoorAirQuality DigitalTwin Exemplar](https://github.com/derlehner/IndoorAirQuality_DigitalTwin_Exemplar)























Fig. 2. Context diagram of the system.


a commercial digital twin framework, while the others are
more ad-hoc.

In this exemplar, similar design choices (e.g. use of containerization) are also made, and we utilize a more or less
ad-hoc approach rather than using a digital twin framework.
The case itself does stem from a different domain, it is more
logistics and cyber-physical systems related, which in our
opinion makes it a good addition to these existing exemplars.


III. L AB S CALE G ANTRY C RANE


The lab scale gantry crane is a small gantry crane system
inspired by the harbor cranes in the port of Antwerp. It moves
small pucks which represents containers from one location to
another.
In what follows, we use the C4 Model [5] to visualize and
describe the architecture of the system with a context and
container diagram. Although it’s tailored for software architectures, we still find it applicable for the gantry crane system.


_A. Architecture_

block_3_judge.reasoning: The extraction accurately describes the CAD models, kinematic model, and the specific data logging setup. It correctly identifies the database type (TimescaleDB) and the logging application's function, all of which are explicitly detailed in the source text.

The extraction states 'Not in Document', but the source text explicitly describes the physical actuators, including motors, controllers, and an electromagnet. Quotes: 'Two Motors and associated Controllers, one to control the cart’s movement, one to control the hoisting movement', 'An Electromagnet at the end of the hoist to pick up containers'.

Correctly identifies the Swing Angle Encoder and Anemometer along with their specific measurement functions (swinging motion, wind speed). The addition of 'for environmental monitoring' is an interpretative label not explicitly found in the text, but the core technical details are accurate.

The description is an excellent match for the source text. It captures the specific functionality of the Continuous Validation Service, including the comparison of trajectories, the calculation of distance metrics, and the threshold-based evaluation mechanism.

block_4_docs_retrieved:
III. L AB S CALE G ANTRY C RANE


The lab scale gantry crane is a small gantry crane system
inspired by the harbor cranes in the port of Antwerp. It moves
small pucks which represents containers from one location to
another.
In what follows, we use the C4 Model [5] to visualize and
describe the architecture of the system with a context and
container diagram. Although it’s tailored for software architectures, we still find it applicable for the gantry crane system.


_A. Architecture_


Figure 2 shows the context diagram of the system. In
essence, it shows the high-level abstraction of what a digital
twin is, and two actors that interact with the system. The
operator is the actor that would operate and monitor this
system day to day, whilst the researcher can perform digital
twin research on it.
Figure 3 shows the container [6] diagram of the system. We
have limited ourselves to the most necessary relationships to
keep the diagram readable.
The gantry crane system, which can operate without digital
twin if needed, consists of five containers.
**Physical Crane:** the actual hardware of the crane.


5 [https://c4model.com/](https://c4model.com/)
6 Not a Docker container, a container is an abstraction in the C4 model for
something that must be there for the system to work.


Fig. 3. Container diagram of the system.



**Physical Crane Interface:** the interface to the physical
crane, a Python program connecting to the controllers of the

crane.

**Control Application:** a Python application that uses the
crane interface to control the crane.

**Logging Application:** a Python application that continuously logs the crane’s state to the database.
**Human Machine Interface:** a Python front-end for the
operator to configure and control the crane.
The digital twin consists of six containers. We’ll describe
them bottom up.

**Database:** a TimescaleDB database that contains all the data

created by the system and its various services.
**Dashboard:** a Grafana dashboard that visualizes data from

the database.

**Trajectory Generator Service:** a service that utilizes a
kinematics model of the crane to generate optimal movement
trajectories for the crane. Such optimal trajectories have no
residual swinging motion at the end.
**Simulation Service:** a service that implements a simulation
of the crane and it’s controllers. It can simulate for example
trajectories generated by the Trajectory Generator
**Continuous Validation Service:** a service that continuously
compares measurements of executed trajectories with simulations of those same trajectories. It calculates distance metrics
between them and compares the value with an acceptable
threshold. In case the threshold is breached operators are
notified via the dashboard and can take appropriate action.
**MQTT Broker:** an MQTT broker for signaling between
services.

Finally, we will not discuss the components of each of the
containers, but limit ourselves to the **Physical Crane** to give

It correctly notes that specific technical details like frequencies and data types are not present. Evidence includes: 'Physical Crane Interface: the interface to the physical crane, a Python program connecting to the controllers of the crane' and 'Logging Application: a Python application that continuously logs the crane’s state to the database'.

The description correctly maps the control flow from the Trajectory Generator Service through the Control Application and Physical Crane Interface to the physical crane, noting the use of MQTT for signaling. It accurately identifies the absence of specific command details and validation mechanisms. Supporting quotes: 'Trajectory Generator Service: a service that utilizes a kinematics model of the crane to generate optimal movement trajectories' and 'Control Application: a Python application that uses the crane interface to control the crane'.

The extraction correctly lists the communication protocols and tools (MQTT Broker, TimescaleDB, Grafana) and accurately states that infrastructure details like topology and security are not provided. This is strongly grounded in the text: 'MQTT Broker: an MQTT broker for signaling between services' and 'Database: a TimescaleDB database'.

The extraction is highly specific and accurate, correctly identifying the on-premise personal computer deployment and the use of Docker Compose. It correctly references Figure 5 and the docker compose file. Supported by quotes: 'plug in the physical crane to a personal computer, and run all of the software on that computer.', 'A docker compose file is provided to bring the containers up and down with a single command.'

block_5_docs_retrieved:
_B. GreenhouseDT_


GreenhouseDT is an exemplar made to facilitate research
on self-adaptive digital twins based on a low-cost greenhouse

[12]. It features behavioral adaptation of both the actual
system (the greenhouse) and the digital twin. The exemplar
and its accompanying documentation is hosted on GitHub [3] . It
relies on commonly used technologies such as an InfluxDB
database, an ActiveMQ message broker and virtual machines
for easy installation. The virtual machine images come with
prerecorded data.


_C. Air Quality Management_


The Air Quality Management exemplar was created to
research challenges in the field of Model-Driven digital twin
engineering [13]. It focuses on a construction engineering case
of indoor air quality management in the context of virus spread
prevention. Three example applications of the digital twin are
provided: runtime visualization, simulation and prediction. The
exemplar and instructions on how to set it up are hosted on
GitHub [4] . It features a Raspberry-Pi with a set of sensors for the
actual system and an AutomationML based digital twin [14].
Microsoft Azure tools are used to implement the presented
digital twin pipeline.


_D. Discussion_


The three exemplars demonstrate the use of digital twins
in the robotics, agricultural and construction domains. They
commonly use single board computers such as the RaspberryPi and containerization or virtualization technologies for easy
dissemination of the code. Interestingly, only one of them uses


2 [https://github.com/cau-se/ARCHES-PiCar-X](https://github.com/cau-se/ARCHES-PiCar-X)
3 [https://github.com/smolang/GreenhouseDT](https://github.com/smolang/GreenhouseDT)
4 [https://github.com/derlehner/IndoorAirQuality DigitalTwin Exemplar](https://github.com/derlehner/IndoorAirQuality_DigitalTwin_Exemplar)























Fig. 2. Context diagram of the system.


a commercial digital twin framework, while the others are
more ad-hoc.

In this exemplar, similar design choices (e.g. use of containerization) are also made, and we utilize a more or less
ad-hoc approach rather than using a digital twin framework.
The case itself does stem from a different domain, it is more
logistics and cyber-physical systems related, which in our
opinion makes it a good addition to these existing exemplars.


III. L AB S CALE G ANTRY C RANE


The lab scale gantry crane is a small gantry crane system
inspired by the harbor cranes in the port of Antwerp. It moves
small pucks which represents containers from one location to
another.
In what follows, we use the C4 Model [5] to visualize and
describe the architecture of the system with a context and
container diagram. Although it’s tailored for software architectures, we still find it applicable for the gantry crane system.


_A. Architecture_
